Designing TV
for five
countries.

2.5 years as Lead Product Designer at the largest Scandinavian streaming company — responsible for live TV, TV Guide, and the design system powering all five markets. The challenge: one interface, five legal realities.

The core
challenge

The same button — "Watch live" — had to behave differently depending on which country you were in, what rights the broadcaster had sold, and whether nPVR was legally available. Before I joined, there were five separate designs solving this. I built one.

01 — Context

Five countries. Five legal realities.

Broadcasting rights in Scandinavia are not uniform. What's legally allowed in Sweden may be prohibited in Norway and only partially permitted in Finland. The platform had to adapt to these rules at the component level — not through separate codebases, but through a single design system with market-specific properties.

🇳🇴 Norway

02 — TV Guide & Live

The TV Guide — timeline at scale.

The TV Guide was the most complex surface I worked on. It needed to work as a timeline across all platforms (web, mobile, Smart TV), adapt to each market's channel list, show live/upcoming/past states clearly, and support nPVR recording actions — where legally permitted.

NDA
Timeline navigation · channel picker · Smart TV
NDA
Logged in + nPVR available · CTA states
NDA
CTA button logic · Post / Ongoing / Future

One of the hardest parts was designing the CTA button logic. The same programme card needed to show different actions depending on three axes simultaneously: timing (past / live now / future), user auth state (logged in / out), and market rights (nPVR available or not).

Timing
Not logged in
Logged in · no nPVR
Logged in · nPVR available
Past broadcast
Play (if catch-up)
Play · Add to list
Play · Add to list · Delete recording
Live now
Watch live
Watch live · Set reminder
Watch live · Record now · Record series
Future
Set reminder
Set reminder
Set reminder · Schedule record
03 — Series & Episodes

Episode lists across platforms.

Series pages had to unify two states: current episodes (available now) and upcoming episodes (pre-release). Each required different sorting logic, different filter options, and different action buttons — adapted per market and per auth state.

NDA
Episode list · mobile · filter & sort states
NDA
Current & upcoming episodes · web
The design
system problem

Before the unified library, each country had its own Figma file, its own component set, its own interpretation of the same UI pattern. A button looked slightly different in Norway than in Sweden. Not intentionally — just because no one had built a system to prevent it.

04 — Design System

One library. Five markets.

I built and led the ONE DESIGN LIBRARY — a unified Figma component library connected to GitHub via design tokens. Every component had market-specific properties that could be toggled per country. The visual output was identical across all five markets; the behaviour adapted to local legal requirements.

NDA
ONE DESIGN LIBRARY · Figma → GitHub structure
NDA
Scenario 1 & 2 · channel picker · web
NDA
Favourite channels · discovery & research docs
What I built
  • Unified component library — Web, iOS, Android, TV
  • Design tokens connected to GitHub via Storybook
  • Market-specific component variants (legal properties)
  • ONE MOTION LIBRARY — animation standards
  • Functional documentation per component
  • Governance process for 9-designer team
What existed before
  • 5 separate Figma files — one per country
  • No shared component library — all duplicated
  • Inconsistent button styles across markets
  • No token system — hardcoded values in code
  • No documentation — knowledge lived in people
  • New designers took months to get productive
05 — Key Decisions

What I decided and why.

One component, market properties — not five components
The instinct was to create separate button components for each market. I pushed back: that would mean maintaining 5 versions of every component across 4 platforms — 20 parallel tracks for a single button. Instead I designed a property system where one component had boolean flags per country (nPVR: true/false, Download: true/false). One source of truth, market-specific outputs.
Architecture · Scalability
Figma connected to GitHub — not exported manually
Design tokens were previously communicated to engineering via Slack messages or Confluence pages. I connected the Figma library directly to GitHub through Storybook, so token updates flowed automatically. This was the same principle as the parcel tracker pipeline — but at enterprise scale, with 9 designers and multiple engineering teams.
"Before this, I'd update a colour and it would take 3 weeks to reach all platforms. Now it's a PR." — Frontend lead, post-launch
Process · Design-dev handoff
CTA scroll behaviour — what's visible depends on context
On programme pages, the action buttons (Play, Record, Add to list) had to scroll and re-appear intelligently based on where the user was in the page. I designed 3 scroll states and a priority system: primary action always visible, secondary actions collapse into a "more" menu when space runs out. This prevented the button bar from pushing content off-screen on smaller devices.
Interaction design · Progressive disclosure
Favourite channels — research before redesign
Adding favourite channels (edit/create/navigation) sounds simple. It wasn't. Navigation patterns differ drastically between mobile, web, and Smart TV. I ran a full discovery process with UXR, a 6-hat analysis session, and concept testing before touching the design. The feature affected every market, every platform, and the TV guide navigation. Getting it wrong would have meant 20 broken flows across 5 countries.
"We need to add favourite channels" was one sentence in a ticket. The spec ended up being 40 pages." — PO, retrospective
Research · Cross-platform complexity
06 — Outcomes

What it added up to.

5→1
Figma files consolidated
One library replacing five separate country files
4
Platforms from one system
Web · iOS · Android · Smart TV — one component library
9
Designers using the system
Across multiple product streams and markets
What I'm most proud of
The market property system. It meant a designer in Stockholm and a designer in Helsinki were working from the same component — and the legal difference between their markets was encoded in the component itself, not in 5 separate files they'd have to keep in sync manually.
What I'd do differently
I'd start the governance documentation earlier. For the first year, the system was maintained by knowledge in my head. When team members changed, onboarding took too long because the rationale behind decisions wasn't written down. I fixed this in year 2 — but year 1 was hard.
07 — Reflection

2.5 years.

This was the job where I stopped thinking about design as screens and started thinking about it as systems. You can't design five markets by designing five screens. You have to design a system that can express the difference between a Norwegian legal constraint and a Finnish one without anyone having to redraw anything.

The hardest part wasn't the complexity. It was convincing people that investing in a design system was worth delaying features. It was. Every sprint after the library launched was faster than the sprint before it.

Working on live TV meant the product was always on. Changes to the TV guide affected millions of viewers watching live right now. That shaped how I approach shipping: incrementally, with rollback plans, with a deep respect for what's already working.

The favourite channels feature took 3 months of research before a single screen was designed. The nPVR CTA logic took 6 weeks of spec work before anything was built. That pace frustrated stakeholders sometimes. But every one of those decisions shipped without a major rollback — in five countries, on four platforms, for millions of users simultaneously.

The lesson
from 2.5 years

Design systems are not deliverables. They're arguments — made in code and components — about how a product should behave. The best argument I made in 2.5 years was: one component, five legal realities, zero separate files. That argument is still running in production.

Anna Izotova · Lead Product Designer · 2026 ← Back to portfolio
